annotations.text: On the basis of this patient’s history and findings on physical examination, I cannot distinguish between syphilis and Crohn’s disease.
Both diseases cause proctitis, fissures, and perianal papules.
On the basis of the patient’s sexual history, syphilis is more likely than inflammatory bowel disease.
In a review of cases in which MSM presented to a surgery clinic for treatment of proctitis, syphilis was a more common cause than Crohn’s disease.
the presence of other anorectal abnormalities in this patient raises doubts about whether he really had hemorrhoids.
If we rule out all these entities from our differential diagnosis, we are left with Crohn’s disease and a number of infectious diseases.
Between January 2013 and January 2014, the number of reported cases of syphilis increased by 15%, and in 2014, a total of 83% of men with primary and secondary syphilis were in the MSM population.
The onset of secondary syphilis typically occurs 4 to 12 weeks after exposure; although symptoms typically resolve in 3 to 12 weeks, they may last for months.
However, rash may go unnoticed by either a patient or a physician.
When considering the diagnosis of tuberculosis, the timeline is usually not helpful because the onset of symptoms can vary.

tubercular proctitis is an extremely rare cause of proctitis that is typically associated with systemic symptoms and is not usually associated with concurrent anal tuberculosis, which represents only 1% of all cases of gastrointestinal tuberculosis.
Because this diagnosis would rely on the coexistence of two very rare forms of tuberculosis, it seems unlikely.
Syphilitic proctitis is a diagnosis that fits this patient’s presentation and timeline.
In the era before penicillin, secondary syphilis was a common cause of proctitis and of a variety of perianal findings, including fissures and condylomata lata, which can mimic external hemorrhoids.
Overall, the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
In order to establish the diagnosis of syphilis in this patient, I would perform a treponemal-specific serologic test for syphilis.
If that test was positive, I would perform a rapid plasma reagin test.
The symptoms in this patient most likely reflect secondary syphilis.

Physical and endoscopic examination may reveal snail-track ulcers that mimic Crohn’s disease, warty lesions that mimic condylomata acuminata, rectal masses that mimic cancer, and colitis that mimics inflammatory bowel disease (as in this case).
This case shows a central issue with the diagnosis of sexually transmitted infections: it can be difficult to identify a causative agent on the basis of history and physical examination alone.

among MSM, even mild or ambiguous symptoms should prompt comprehensive testing, including urinary nucleic acid testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia and also rectal and pharyngeal culture and nucleic acid testing.
Furthermore, MSM should be screened for sexually transmitted infections at regular intervals, even when they have no symptoms.
The two most common reasons for missing a treatable cause of anal symptoms are that patients attribute their symptoms to hemorrhoids and that doctors believe them.
I counseled him about the prevention and treatment of sexually transmitted infections and initiated preexposure prophylaxis for HIV with the combination of emtricitabine and tenofovir.
